role_summary: Owner of Jean's Beauty Shop at 510 Mobile Street in Hattiesburg, Mississippi; co-founder (April 1964) and Executive Secretary of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party (MFDP); Treasurer of the 1964 Freedom Summer project in Hattiesburg; member of the August 1964 MFDP delegation to the Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City.
ai_reading: Connor's top semantic neighbor is Charles McLaurin (entry 17) at a high cosine count of 211, a strong single-neighbor signature in this catalog. The McLaurin pairing is the catalog's clearest example of a Hattiesburg-Hattiesburg-and-Sunflower-County Mississippi cross-region cosine: Connor's Mobile Street community-business-into-organizing trajectory and McLaurin's Sunflower County voter-registration field work both fall under the broader Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party network, and the embedding catches that shared MFDP-and-COFO institutional vocabulary as a stronger signal than either's pairing with the Mississippi-headquarters or national SNCC voices. The novel observation her page surfaces is the local-business-owner-as-organizer signature: Connor's beauty shop was the physical infrastructure of the Hattiesburg movement (the SNCC office set up next door; Mobile Street's businesses were the community gathering places that anchored the organizing), an infrastructure-of-community-business reading the catalog mostly leaves implicit in other Mississippi pages.
biographical_paragraph: While Connor's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), her external biographical record is well-documented in the Mississippi Encyclopedia and the SNCC Digital Gateway [src: 1, src: 2]. Connor was born October 29, 1932 to John Henry and Esther Gould in Hattiesburg, Mississippi's Mobile Street community, the city's historically African American district. She attended Eureka High School, the city's second modern brick school for African American students. Connor owned Jean's Beauty Shop at 510 Mobile Street, and after SNCC organizers established an office near her business she became one of the central Hattiesburg organizers [src: 2]. In April 1964 she was a founding member of the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party and served as chairperson of her precinct (the "library precinct" in Hattiesburg) and across the project as Executive Secretary of the MFDP [src: 2]. From June through August 1964 she served as an officer for the Hattiesburg chapter of the Council of Federated Organizations (COFO) and as Treasurer of the Hattiesburg Freedom Summer project; she joined the MFDP delegation to the August 1964 Democratic National Convention in Atlantic City and was among the delegates who refused the two-seat compromise the Johnson administration offered the MFDP, returning under Klan threats and intimidation [src: 2].

sources.title: Peggy Jean Connor oral history
